subtitle: First of the Microbe Hunters
summary: Leeuwenhoek discovers multiple kinds of tiny creatures in rainwater and is astonished by their size and movement. He designs clever experiments to prove they do not fall from the sky, and accidentally discovers that soaking pepper in water breeds enormous numbers of them. When he writes to the Royal Society in London, they are skeptical, but eventually Robert Hooke confirms his findings and Leeuwenhoek is made a Fellow, though he stubbornly refuses to reveal the secrets of his best lenses.

segments.anchor_sentence: That man was so unashamed of his admirations and his surprises at a nature full of startling events and impossible things.

visual.scene_description: Leeuwenhoek hunched over his tiny brass microscope, peering into a glass tube of water and discovering multiple kinds of tiny creatures swimming about, his face lit with astonishment.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, round-faced Dutch man with big nose and white curled periwig in long dark wool coat, hunched over tiny brass plate microscope held to his eye, glass tube of water on wooden table, 17th-century Dutch study with leaded glass window, circular magnified inset showing four distinct tiny creatures of different sizes swimming with thin hairlike legs and spinning motions

segments.anchor_sentence: But where did these outlandish little inhabitants of the rainwater come from?

visual.scene_description: Leeuwenhoek stands outside in the rain holding a large blue-glazed porcelain dish up on a box to collect pure rainwater, with his Dutch house and eaves-trough visible behind him, conducting his experiment to determine where the tiny creatures originate.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, round-faced Dutch man with big nose and white curled periwig in long dark wool coat, standing in falling rain beside a brick Dutch house, holding a large blue-glazed porcelain dish on top of a wooden box to catch raindrops, eaves-trough and cobblestone yard visible, determined concentrated expression, grey overcast sky with diagonal rain streaks

segments.anchor_sentence: Did he write to the Royal Society to tell them of this entirely unsuspected world of life he had discovered?

visual.scene_description: Leeuwenhoek at his cluttered study table with peppercorns soaking in water in a glass vessel, peering through his lens at the pepper water and finding it teeming with an incredible number of tiny animals.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, round-faced Dutch man with big nose and white curled periwig in long dark wool coat, seated at wooden table with scattered black peppercorns and glass vessel of murky pepper-infused water, holding tiny brass plate microscope to his eye, fine needles on table, circular magnified inset showing dense swarm of rod-shaped and round tiny organisms tumbling in all directions

segments.anchor_sentence: And now to write all this to the great men off there in London!

visual.scene_description: Leeuwenhoek sits writing a long letter at his desk by candlelight, pages covered in neat handwriting and arithmetic calculations, looking frustrated and proud as he defends his discoveries to the doubting Royal Society.
visual.animation_prompt: Simple linocut print style illustration, bold black outlines, flat muted colors in dusty rose and sage green and ochre, rough textured edges, hand-printed look on natural paper, children's book illustration, round-faced Dutch man with big nose and white curled periwig in long dark wool coat, seated at wooden writing desk by candlelight, quill pen in hand, multiple pages spread out covered in neat rows of arithmetic, wax-sealed letter nearby, determined furrowed brow, 17th-century Dutch study with brass microscopes visible in wooden cabinet behind him

segments.anchor_sentence: So the Royal Society commissioned Robert Hooke and Nehemiah Grew to build the very best microscopes, and brew pepper water from the finest quality of black pepper.

visual.scene_description: Leeuwenhoek proudly but guardedly showing his microscope to the visiting Doctor Molyneux in his study lined with cabinets of hundreds of tiny microscopes, letting the Englishman look but hovering protectively over his instruments.
